When a pulse of tracer is introduced into the feed, the RTD function is mathematically expressed as the normal­ ized tracer concentration in the output (Fogler 2016; Bha­ lode et al. 2021): E (t) = Cout (t) ∫∞ 0 Cout (t) dt  (1) where E (t) represents the RTD function, and Cout (t) denotes the tracer concentration at the outlet. An alternative representation of RTD is the cumulative distribution function, F(t), defined as: F (t) = ∫ t o E (t) dt or E (t) = dF (t) dt   (2) where F(t) indicates the fraction of materials that have remained in the process for a duration of t or less (Fogler 2016). Figure 1 illustrates the typical RTD responses for ideal PFRs and CSTRs following pulse or step tracer injec­ tion. For a pulse injection, E(t) can be directly obtained from Eq. (1). However, for a step injection, E(t) is derived from the cumulative function using: E (t) = dF (t) dt = d dt [Cout (t) Co ]   (3) where Co is the tracer concentration in the feed after step injection. caption: Figure 2. illustrates representative RTD profiles for different reactor models, highlighting their distinct characteristics. Once experimental RTD data are obtained, an appropriate model can be selected by compar­ ing the measured RTD with theoretical predictions. Since CM involves multiple interconnected unit opera­ tions, the RTD of the entire process should also be consid­ ered. If the RTD of individual unit operations is known, the overall RTD can be computed using the convolution tech­ nique. Mathematically, the convolution of two RTD func­ tions is defined as: E1 (t) ⊗E2 (t) = ∫ t 0 E1 (t −θ ) E2 (θ ) dθ   (16) For a manufacturing process consisting of k unit operations, the overall RTD is given by: Etotal (t) = Eunit 1 (t) ⊗Eunit2 (t) ⊗. . . ⊗Eunit k (t)  (17) where Eunitk(t) represents the RTD of the k-th unit operation. E (t) = { 0 for t < τ P F R e−(t−τ P F R)/τ CST R τ CST R for t ≥τ CST R   (12) where τ P F R and τ CST R are the mean residence times of the PFR and CSTR, respectively. For a TIS model consisting of multiple CSTRs with equal volume, the RTD function is given by: E (t) = tn−1 (n −1)!( n τ ) n e−nt/τ   (13) where n is the number of CSTRs in series, and τ is the mean residence time of each CSTR. Another widely used model to describe non-ideal flow is the axial dispersion model, which accounts for both the con­ vective and dispersive transport of materials. The governing equation (Badman et al. 2019; Bhalode et al. 2021) is: ∂C(t, z) ∂t = D∂2C(t, z) ∂z2 −v ∂C(t, z) ∂z   (14) where D is the dispersion coefficient, and v is the superficial velocity in the axial direction. description: This figure presents a series of plots illustrating various Residence Time Distribution (RTD) profiles for different reactor models. Each panel (a through e) depicts the evolution of the RTD function E(t) over time. Panel (a) shows a step function representing the RTD in a perfectly mixed flow reactor (PFR). Panels (b) and (c) display exponential decay patterns typical of a continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR). Panel (d) compares the RTD profiles for different numbers of CSTRs in series (n=2 to n=10), demonstrating how increasing the number of CSTRs narrows the distribution peak. Finally, panel (e) shows a broader distribution indicative of a tank-in-series (TIS) model. These plots collectively highlight the impact of reactor configuration on the RTD and aid in selecting the most appropriate model for a given process.

caption: Figure 3. provides an example of combining three indi­ vidual unit operations to generate a unified RTD profile for the entire process. This approach is particularly valu­ able in CM, where a large number of subunits contribute to the overall RTD. By leveraging the combined RTD profile, manufacturers can assess how upstream process variations impact downstream product quality. Additionally, multiple diversion points can be designed using real-time PAT sen­ sors to enhance process control and maintain consistent product quality (Bhaskar and Singh 2019; Bhalode et al. 2021). Application of RTD to pharmaceutical processes In this review, a comprehensive analysis of RTD charac­ terization and its application in continuous pharmaceuti­ cal manufacturing are provided, with a particular focus on OSD production involving solid particles. While CM is also being implemented in other pharmaceutical areas, such as drug substance synthesis (Baumann et al. 2020) and biolog­ ics production (Godawat et al. 2015; Somasundaram et al. 2018), the focus here is on OSD manufacturing due to the inherent complexity and heterogeneity of material flow and particle interactions. Typical OSD manufacturing processes involve multiple unit operations, including blending, granu­ lation, milling, compression, and encapsulation, among oth­ ers. To accurately assess process dynamics and their impact on product quality, RTD must be thoroughly characterized not only within individual critical unit operations but also across the entire integrated manufacturing process. The following sections will further elaborate on these aspects,
description: The figure illustrates the combination of three individual unit operations—feeder, blender, and granulator—to generate a unified residence time distribution (RTD) profile for the entire process. Each unit operation is represented by a graph showing the RTD over time. The feeder and blender have sharp peaks indicating rapid mixing, while the granulator shows a broader distribution suggesting a slower, more extended process. The convolution of these individual RTD functions results in a composite RTD curve, demonstrating how the combined effect of these operations influences the overall process dynamics.

caption: Figure 4. presents a funnel plot derived from the RTD function of a typical TIS model, which is then con­ voluted with the inlet concentration profile from the LIW feeder to predict the outlet concentration of the mixer. The plot highlights that, within a specific disturbance magnitude and duration, the output concentration remains within the target assay range (100 ± 10%). Deviations beyond this region generally result in out-of-specification products. It is important to note that when blend homogeneity must be monitored, other quality metrics, such as RSD, can also be used to assess variability in composition over time (Pern­ enkil and Cooney 2006). Overall, this approach facilitates risk-based disturbance management by supporting real-time decision-making during CM operation, allowing for the effective diversion of non-conforming material (ICH Q13 2023). Continuous granulation While high-shear and fluid-bed granulation remain standard in the pharmaceutical industry, twin-screw wet granulation (TSWG) has emerged as a key unit operation, enabling the CM of granulated products. Granulation enhances flowabil­ ity, compactability, and content uniformity, ensuring that downstream tableting and encapsulation can proceed with­ out quality-related risks. In TSWG, a blend is continuously fed into a granulator, where two co-rotating screws generate convective and shear forces, interacting with a binder liquid to form granules with the desired product attributes, such as granule size, porosity, compressibility, and dissolution kinet­ ics (Dahlgren et al. 2019; Thompson 2015). caption: Figure 6. As the impeller speed increases, the peak of RTD curves appears earlier, indicating an accelerated liquid circulation in the reactor and improved mixing performance. Multiple peaks are observed in RTD curves under all impeller speeds expect at 0 rpm. This behavior is attributed to the circulation flow induced by the draft tube in the reactor. Driven by the impeller, the fluid flows downward inside the draft tube and upward outside, re-entering from the top to form a stable loop. Under laminar flow conditions, the tracer undergoes multiple circulation cycles before exiting the reactor, resulting in multiple peaks in the RTD curves. As the impeller speed increases, the circulation becomes faster, which reduces residence time differences and shortens the intervals between successive peaks. Additionally, enhanced mixing at higher impeller speeds reduces both the number and amplitude of the RTD peaks. In Figure 7, the number of tanks in series is plotted against impeller speed for both constant and variable viscosity conditions. In both cases, the number of tanks in series decreases with increasing impeller speed, except at 0 rpm under variable viscosity.This trend reflects improved mixing performance, which gradually approaches that of an ideal reactor. However, at higher impeller speeds, the rate of improvement diminishes due to limitations in mixing enhancement under laminar flow. At 0 rpm, the number of tanks in series is less than 1 under variable viscosity conditions, indicating the presence of dead zones or short-circuiting flows.

caption: Figure 6. As the impeller speed increases, the peak of RTD curves appears earlier, indicating an accelerated liquid circulation in the reactor and improved mixing performance. Multiple peaks are observed in RTD curves under all impeller speeds expect at 0 rpm. This behavior is attributed to the circulation flow induced by the draft tube in the reactor. Driven by the impeller, the fluid flows downward inside the draft tube and upward outside, re-entering from the top to form a stable loop. Under laminar flow conditions, the tracer undergoes multiple circulation cycles before exiting the reactor, resulting in multiple peaks in the RTD curves. As the impeller speed increases, the circulation becomes faster, which reduces residence time differences and shortens the intervals between successive peaks. Additionally, enhanced mixing at higher impeller speeds reduces both the number and amplitude of the RTD peaks. In Figure 7, the number of tanks in series is plotted against impeller speed for both constant and variable viscosity conditions. In both cases, the number of tanks in series decreases with increasing impeller speed, except at 0 rpm under variable viscosity.This trend reflects improved mixing performance, which gradually approaches that of an ideal reactor. However, at higher impeller speeds, the rate of improvement diminishes due to limitations in mixing enhancement under laminar flow. At 0 rpm, the number of tanks in series is less than 1 under variable viscosity conditions, indicating the presence of dead zones or short-circuiting flows.

caption: Figure 9. With increasing space time, the peaks in the RTD curves are delayed and reduced in height, indicating a longer average residence time. This leads to a large difference in residence times along different flow paths, resulting in a broader RTD curve and more significant tailing. For example, under constant viscosity conditions, at a space time of 45 s, the RTD curve (E(t)) becomes nearly zero around 200 s, indicating nearly all fluid has been replaced by the tracer. description: Figure 11 illustrates the effects of viscosity on the Residence Time Distribution (RTD) in a stirred tank reactor (STR). Panel A shows the RTD for constant viscosity fluids at different viscosity levels (1 Pa·s, 10 Pa·s, 100 Pa·s, and 1000 Pa·s). Increasing viscosity shifts the RTD peaks to the right and increases their height, indicating delayed residence times and stronger concentration peaks. Panel B depicts the RTD for variable viscosity fluids described by Equations 19, 20, and 21. Under variable viscosity conditions, the RTD curves exhibit more fluctuations compared to constant viscosity, suggesting poorer mixing performance. The impeller speed is fixed at 375 rpm, and the reactor type is a stirred tank with a draft tube inner diameter of 310 mm and a reactor diameter of 476 mm.

description: This figure depicts the effect of viscosity on the residence time distribution (RTD) in a single-stage stirred tank reactor (STR) operating at 80 rpm and a residence time of 180 seconds. The graph shows three different viscosity conditions: a constant viscosity case (black line) and two variable viscosity cases (green and blue lines) labeled with n = 2.20. The y-axis represents the normalized residence time distribution, while the x-axis represents time in seconds. As viscosity increases, the RTD peaks shift to the right and become broader, indicating a slower flow and reduced circulation efficiency. This suggests that higher viscosity leads to poorer mixing performance and a more complex flow pattern within the reactor.

caption: Figure 16. Effect of viscosity on RTD in two-stage STRs (N = 80 rpm, τ = 180 s). 4. Conclusions In this paper, a simplified viscosity model based on the mean age of the fluid was proposed to describe viscosity evolution during polymerization. The residence time distri- bution (RTD) of the Newtonian fluids and the variable-viscosity fluids in the stirred tank reactors equipped with the draft tubes was investigated numerically. The results demon- strated that considering viscosity evolution is essential to capture abnormal flow behaviors such as dead zones and short-circuiting, underscoring the importance of incorporating viscosity variation for accurate RTD prediction in polymerization reactors. The study also showed that impeller speed and space time are the dominant factors controlling the RTD in laminar stirred tanks, whereas viscosity has a relatively minor effect. The comparison between single- and two-stage configurations revealed that the second stage significantly improves mixing uniformity, smooths RTD curves, and mitigates the influence of viscosity differences. Beyond reproducing flow characteristics, these findings emphasize the significance of the proposed simplified viscosity model. By linking viscosity to the mean age of the fluid, the model provides an efficient way to capture the effect of viscosity evolution on RTD without introducing excessive complexity. This approach provides practical guidance for reactor design, scale-up, and operation in polymerization processes. Since the effect of shear stress on viscosity was not considered, the proposed model is applicable mainly to Boger-type fluids, which are insensitive to shear stress. In future work, we will extend the model to incorporate the effect of shear stress on viscosity, in order to more accurately capture viscosity variations in polymerization reactors.
description: The figure illustrates the effect of viscosity on the residence time distribution (RTD) in a two-stage stirred tank reactor (STR) configuration. The x-axis represents time (t) in seconds, ranging from 0 to 500 s. The y-axis represents the normalized residence time distribution value. Despite varying viscosity conditions, the RTD curves remain nearly identical across different viscosity models, indicating a negligible impact of viscosity on the RTD. This suggests that the second stage of the STR significantly improves mixing uniformity and mitigates the influence of viscosity differences, leading to consistent RTD profiles.

description: The figure illustrates the effect of viscosity on the residence time distribution (RTD) in a two-stage stirred tank reactor (STR) with a draft tube. Three different viscosity models are compared: Eq. 19 (black squares), Eq. 20 (red circles), and Eq. 21 (blue triangles). The curves peak around the same value and decline similarly, indicating that the viscosity models have a negligible impact on the RTD.
